metadata.summary: Nielsen's Heuristic #3: provide clearly marked emergency exits (undo, cancel, back, close, reset) so users recover from mistakes without extended processes.

content.title: User Control and Freedom
content.summary: Nielsen's Usability Heuristic #3: every interface must provide clearly marked "emergency exits" — undo, cancel, back, close, and reset — so users can recover from mistakes without going through extended processes. Prefer undo over confirmation dialogs for reversible actions; reserve confirmation only for irreversible, high-stakes operations.

why.p: Users make errors. Interfaces that trap users or impose permanent consequences destroy trust and cause abandonment. Forgiving design lowers the cognitive cost of exploration and reduces fear of mistakes, which directly increases engagement and task completion rates.

list.item: Heuristic audit of a feature spec or UI for missing undo/cancel/exit mechanisms
Generating a User Control Audit report (action table with undo/cancel/exit columns)
Code review: checking that destructive backend actions have soft-delete or undo hooks
Accessibility review: verifying keyboard escape paths and focus management in modal flows
Design review of multi-step wizards or onboarding flows for trapped-user patterns
As a substitute for actual usability testing — structural absence of controls is detectable, but whether users feel trapped requires behavioral data
Complex undo architecture in database-backed systems — engineering judgment on consistency and rollback scope is required
When the system genuinely cannot support undo (sent emails, executed financial transactions) — the design solution (clear warnings) needs human decision

section.name: control-mechanisms
section.p: Undo — reverse the last action; prefer this over confirmation for reversible operations. Redo — restore an undone action. Cancel — discard current changes and return to previous state. Back — return to previous screen in a flow. Close — exit current context (modal, panel, overlay). Reset — restore default settings.

rule.statement: Prefer undo over confirmation dialogs for reversible actions.
rule.rationale: Undo feels less disruptive, reduces friction for expert users, and does not interrupt the action flow; confirmation dialogs should be reserved for irreversible, high-stakes operations.

section.name: undo-vs-confirmation
section.p: Use undo when: action is frequent, recovery is technically possible, impact is moderate (e.g., deleting a list item). Use confirmation when: action is irreversible, impact is severe, action is rare (e.g., permanently deleting an account, canceling a subscription).

rule.statement: Use soft delete (move to trash/archive) by default for any content deletion; permanent delete requires explicit secondary confirmation.
rule.rationale: Soft delete gives users a recovery window and eliminates accidental permanent data loss without requiring a confirmation dialog on every deletion.

rule.statement: Every modal must have at least three exit paths: X button, Escape key, and clicking outside the modal.
rule.rationale: Users expect all three; implementing only one or two creates trapped-user scenarios for keyboard and mouse users respectively.

example.note: Gmail "Undo send" — action completes immediately, then a dismissible toast offers 5-30 second undo window. Removes the need for a "Are you sure?" dialog before sending.
Newsletter popup with no X button and no close option — user must subscribe or refresh page. Forced modal with no exit is a trapped-user antipattern.

rule.statement: Show step count and provide Back navigation on every step of a multi-step wizard except the first; provide a persistent Exit option throughout.
rule.rationale: Users who cannot see progress or navigate backward feel trapped and abandon; visible progress and exits reduce abandonment in long flows.

rule.statement: Do not use "Are you sure?" confirmations for non-destructive or routine actions.
rule.rationale: Confirmation fatigue causes users to click through without reading; reserve confirmations for genuinely destructive operations only.

rule.statement: Every user-facing action must be assessed for: undo availability, cancel availability, exit mechanism, and recovery method.
rule.rationale: Missing any one of these for a high-impact action is a usability defect; systematic audit prevents gaps.

rule.statement: Flag high-severity gaps as: irreversible action with no confirmation AND no undo.
rule.rationale: The combination of irreversibility and lack of both undo and confirmation is the highest-risk pattern; it must be addressed before shipping.

section.name: accessibility-requirements
section.p: Keyboard shortcuts for undo (Ctrl+Z) and redo (Ctrl+Y/Shift+Z) must be implemented. Escape key must always close modals and dismiss overlays. Focus must return to the triggering element when a modal closes. Undo actions must be announced to screen readers. Cancel and close buttons must have text labels, not icon-only affordances.

antipattern.description: No undo for frequently performed actions.
antipattern.reason: Users who make frequent mistakes in high-volume workflows lose trust quickly when recovery requires more effort than the original action.

antipattern.description: Hidden or hard-to-find Cancel buttons in forms and dialogs.
antipattern.reason: Users who cannot find the exit feel trapped; Cancel must be visually prominent and near the primary action button.

antipattern.description: Confirmation dialog overuse — "Are you sure?" on every action including non-destructive ones.
antipattern.reason: Confirmation fatigue causes users to dismiss dialogs without reading, defeating their purpose for high-stakes actions.

rule.statement: Pair agent audit with axe-core or pa11y run; agent cannot detect focus traps in rendered interfaces.
rule.rationale: Agent identifies structural absence of controls from descriptions; actual keyboard navigation testing requires browser tooling.

rule.statement: Undo and cancel implementation decisions require engineering sign-off; agent audit identifies the need, not the technical approach.
rule.rationale: Undo architecture depends on backend event sourcing, transaction logs, or soft-delete patterns that require engineering judgment on consistency and rollback scope.

rule.statement: Agent may recommend confirmation dialogs for actions where the better solution is undo (more modern, less disruptive); review recommendations against the undo-vs-confirmation heuristic.
rule.rationale: Agents lack nuanced judgment about whether an action is truly irreversible or just perceived as such; human designer judgment calibrates the right mechanism.

list.item: Prefer undo over confirmation for reversible actions — undo feels less disruptive and reduces friction for expert users
Use soft delete (move to trash/archive) by default for any content deletion; permanent delete requires explicit secondary confirmation
Every modal must have at least three exit paths: X button, Escape key, and clicking outside — implement all three, not just one
In multi-step wizards, always show step count and provide Back on every step except the first; an Exit option should persist throughout
Avoid "Are you sure?" confirmations for non-destructive actions — confirmation fatigue causes users to click through without reading
Auto-save + discard-on-exit is safer than Save/Cancel for form editing — removes the decision burden while preserving control

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A Claude subagent receives a list of user-facing actions (e.g., from a feature spec or screen inventory) and outputs a User Control Audit table: each action rated for Undo availability, Cancel availability, Exit mechanism, and Recovery method. It flags gaps as High/Medium/Low severity based on action destructiveness. For each gap, it outputs a specific remediation recommendation drawn from the heuristic patterns (undo toast, soft delete, multi-step exit, confirmation dialog).

rule.statement: When using agents to audit, provide action names in context of their data impact. Agent audit based on action names alone may miss context-dependent severity.
rule.rationale: "Delete" in a shopping cart (trivially reversible) is different from "delete account" (high stakes). Context prevents over-severity rating.

section.name: cli-tools-and-services
section.text:
CLI tools and services for control verification and accessibility testing:
axe-core CLI — Flags missing keyboard escape paths, focus traps in dialogs. Install: npm install -g @axe-core/cli / https://github.com/dequelabs/axe-core
Playwright — Scripted keyboard navigation testing — verify Escape key closes modals, Back button works. Install: npm install -D playwright / https://playwright.dev
jest-axe — Unit-test accessibility (including focus management) in React component tests. Install: npm install -D jest-axe / https://github.com/nickcolley/jest-axe
pa11y — CLI accessibility runner; catches missing dialog close mechanisms. Install: npm install -g pa11y / https://pa11y.org
Figma (SaaS) — Partial agent-friendliness via REST read. Export flow/prototype structure; agent checks for back/cancel linkages in prototype connections.
Deque Axe Monitor (SaaS) — Yes, agent-friendly via API. Automated a11y scanning including focus trap detection; REST API for results.
Hotjar / FullStory (SaaS) — No direct agent API. Rage-click and exit heatmaps reveal where users feel trapped; export data for agent context.
PostHog (OSS/SaaS) — Yes, agent-friendly via REST API. Session recordings + event data; use to identify rage-click patterns on non-cancellable flows.
Sentry (OSS/SaaS) — Yes, agent-friendly via API. If trapped users trigger error states (back button = 404, etc.), Sentry captures these.

antipattern.description: Agent audit based on action names alone may miss context-dependent severity.
antipattern.reason: "Delete" in a shopping cart (trivially reversible) is different from "delete account" (high stakes). Always provide action context.

antipattern.description: Agent cannot detect focus traps in rendered interfaces without accessibility tooling.
antipattern.reason: Always pair agent audit with axe-core or pa11y run. Agent identifies structural absence of controls from descriptions; actual keyboard navigation testing requires browser tooling.

antipattern.description: Assuming undo/cancel implementation decisions are within agent scope.
antipattern.reason: Undo/cancel implementation decisions require engineering sign-off; agent audit identifies the need, not the implementation approach. Undo architecture depends on backend patterns that require engineering judgment.

antipattern.description: Accepting agent recommendation of confirmation dialogs without review.
antipattern.reason: Agent may recommend confirmation dialogs for actions where the better solution is undo (more modern, less disruptive); review recommendations against the undo-vs-confirmation heuristic.

antipattern.description: Trusting agent verification of keyboard shortcuts without browser testing.
antipattern.reason: Keyboard shortcut verification (Escape, Ctrl+Z) requires live browser testing; agent cannot substitute for Playwright or manual keyboard navigation checks.

template.purpose: User Control Audit: actions × undo/cancel/exit/recovery columns + gap severity
Playwright test to verify Escape key closes all modals
